f0_mean.description: The average pitch of your voice during speech, measured in Hertz.
f0_mean.science_summary: Fundamental frequency is one of the most studied vocal biomarkers for emotional state. Acute stress reliably raises F0 by 10-20 Hz, while depression is associated with a lowered, monotone pitch. Prosodic flatness — low F0 variability centered at a low mean — is a consistent finding across depression studies spanning 40+ years.
f0_mean.evidence_citation: Cummins et al., IEEE Signal Process. Mag. 2015; Mundt et al., Annals of Gen. Psychiatry 2012

f0_std.description: How much your pitch fluctuates during speech — a measure of vocal expressiveness.
f0_std.science_summary: Reduced pitch variability (prosodic monotony) is one of the most replicated biomarkers of depression, present in clinician ratings and algorithmic assessments alike. Anxiety states tend to produce elevated and erratic variability. Pitch variability explains up to 30% of variance in PHQ-9 scores in regression models from large-scale naturalistic studies.
f0_std.evidence_citation: Williamson et al., Interspeech 2013; France et al., IEEE TASLP 2000

jitter.description: Cycle-to-cycle variation in vocal cord vibration period — lower means a steadier voice.
jitter.science_summary: Jitter reflects micro-instabilities in laryngeal muscle control, which increase under emotional or physiological stress. Elevated jitter is observed in states of vocal fatigue, acute anxiety, and conditions affecting motor control of the larynx. While not a primary diagnostic marker, it complements other vocal features in multi-feature models of stress detection.
jitter.evidence_citation: Dejonckere et al., Eur. Arch. Otorhinolaryngol. 2001; Kreiman & Sidtis 2011

shimmer.description: Cycle-to-cycle variation in vocal loudness — lower means more stable voice production.
shimmer.science_summary: Shimmer captures instabilities in the amplitude envelope of the voice signal, reflecting irregular glottal closure and vocal fold tension. Increases with vocal fatigue, emotional distress, and conditions affecting laryngeal neuromuscular control. Shimmer combined with jitter and HNR forms a validated triad for clinical voice quality assessment.
shimmer.evidence_citation: Teixeira et al., Procedia Technology 2013; Ferrer et al., Interspeech 2015

hnr.description: The ratio of harmonic (tonal) energy to background noise in your voice — higher means a cleaner, clearer voice.
hnr.science_summary: HNR is a primary measure of voice quality, reflecting how efficiently the vocal folds vibrate. It drops significantly under acute stress and shows sustained reduction in depression, likely due to incomplete glottal closure and altered subglottal pressure. HNR is among the top-5 features in virtually every published vocal biomarker study for depression and stress screening.
hnr.evidence_citation: Menne et al., arXiv 2025; Alghifari et al., Sensors 2024

rms_energy.description: The overall loudness and physical energy of your voice signal.
rms_energy.science_summary: RMS energy captures the acoustic power of speech and correlates with emotional activation and arousal. Depression is consistently associated with reduced vocal energy across multiple cohorts. Anxiety can produce elevated energy due to heightened arousal but with irregular patterns. Energy level is a key feature in prosody-based mood classification models.
rms_energy.evidence_citation: Scherer et al., Interspeech 2015; Schuller et al., AVEC 2013

speech_rate.description: How quickly you speak, measured in syllables per second.
speech_rate.science_summary: Speech rate is among the most robust vocal indicators of psychological state. Depression slows speech rate, often markedly, while anxiety tends to accelerate it. Longitudinal tracking of speech rate in the same individual shows 15-30% changes between depressive and euthymic episodes. It is a feature in FDA-cleared mental health voice biomarker platforms.
speech_rate.evidence_citation: Mundt et al., Annals Gen. Psychiatry 2012; Stivers & Enfield, Psychol. Sci. 2010

spectral_centroid.description: The center of mass of your voice's frequency spectrum — a measure of how bright or dark your voice sounds.
spectral_centroid.science_summary: A higher spectral centroid corresponds to a brighter, more forward-placed voice, while a lower centroid reflects a darker, heavier quality. Vocal fatigue and low-arousal states shift the centroid downward as speakers produce less high-frequency energy. It is used as a secondary feature in computational models linking voice to fatigue and energy.
spectral_centroid.evidence_citation: Ishi et al., Speech Communication 2008; Schuller et al., AVEC 2014

spectral_entropy.description: How random or complex the distribution of energy is across your voice's frequency spectrum.
spectral_entropy.science_summary: Spectral entropy quantifies the disorder of the spectral energy distribution. High entropy indicates noise-like, broadband speech (common in stressed or breathy phonation), while low entropy indicates tonal, structured speech. Current evidence links it to voice quality changes but large-scale clinical validation is still limited, making it a research-grade feature.

zcr.description: How often the voice signal crosses zero amplitude — correlates with high-frequency content and vocal tension.
zcr.science_summary: ZCR is a time-domain proxy for spectral content: higher ZCR indicates more high-frequency energy, which can reflect fricative-heavy speech patterns or tense, pressed phonation under stress. While it is widely used in speech feature sets, its direct link to specific psychological states requires further clinical validation.
zcr.evidence_citation: Quatieri & Malyska, IEEE TASLP 2012; Eyben et al., openSMILE 2010

voice_breaks.description: The number of unvoiced interruptions within otherwise voiced speech segments.
voice_breaks.science_summary: Voice breaks indicate phonation instability — moments where the vocal folds fail to sustain vibration. They increase with laryngeal tension, emotional dysregulation, and vocal fatigue. Elevated voice break counts are observed in acute psychological distress and have been proposed as a feature for detecting emotional load in naturalistic speech.
voice_breaks.evidence_citation: Titze 1994; Laukkanen et al., Logopedics Phoniatrics Vocology 2008

alpha_ratio.description: The ratio of low-frequency to high-frequency vocal energy — the most reliable single acoustic marker for stress.
alpha_ratio.science_summary: The alpha ratio measures the balance between energy below 1 kHz and energy above 1 kHz. Under stress, the larynx tenses and high-frequency energy increases relative to low-frequency energy, lowering the alpha ratio. Menne et al. (2025) identified alpha ratio as the single most predictive feature in a 73-feature vocal stress study (r=0.42, p<0.001), outperforming F0, HNR, and MFCCs in isolation.
alpha_ratio.evidence_citation: Menne et al., arXiv 2025; Sondhi et al., J. Acoust. Soc. Am. 2021

mfcc3.description: The 3rd mel-frequency cepstral coefficient — captures mid-range spectral shape and vocal tract resonance.
mfcc3.science_summary: MFCC3 encodes the spectral shape of the vocal tract at mid-range frequencies, influenced by articulation and laryngeal posture. Research by Menne et al. (2025) found MFCC3 significantly associated with cortisol levels (beta = -0.606, p < 0.01), suggesting it captures physiological stress load independently of other acoustic features. It contributes to multi-feature depression detection models alongside F0 and energy.
mfcc3.evidence_citation: Menne et al., arXiv 2025; Davis & Mermelstein, IEEE TASLP 1980

f1_mean.description: The lowest resonance frequency of your vocal tract, shaped by tongue height and mouth opening.
f1_mean.science_summary: F1 reflects the primary resonance of the vocal tract cavity and is shaped primarily by jaw opening and tongue height. It varies by vowel and speaker anatomy but shows population-level shifts with arousal and articulatory precision. Large-scale clinical validation of F1 as a standalone mental health biomarker is still in early stages.
f1_mean.evidence_citation: Ladefoged & Johnson, A Course in Phonetics 2015; Scherer et al., Interspeech 2015

f2_mean.description: The second resonance of your vocal tract, related to tongue position and articulatory clarity.
f2_mean.science_summary: F2 is influenced by tongue advancement and reflects articulatory precision. In states of cognitive load, fatigue, or dysarthria, F2 patterns become compressed (centralization), indicating reduced articulatory effort. Its utility as a standalone mental health biomarker requires further large-scale validation in healthy populations.
f2_mean.evidence_citation: Nwe et al., Speech Communication 2003; Scherer 2003

spectral_flux.description: How rapidly the spectral content of your voice changes from frame to frame — a measure of vocal animation.
spectral_flux.science_summary: Spectral flux captures the rate of change of the spectral envelope over time, reflecting the dynamic expressiveness of speech. High spectral flux corresponds to animated, rapidly-varying speech associated with high arousal states. It is used as an arousal feature in emotion recognition systems and contributes to distinguishing engaged versus flat/depressed speech styles.
spectral_flux.evidence_citation: Eyben et al., openSMILE 2010; Schuller et al., AVEC 2014

stress_score.description: A 0-100 composite score reflecting overall vocal stress load, derived from multiple acoustic features.
stress_score.science_summary: The stress index integrates alpha ratio, HNR, F0 elevation, jitter, shimmer, and energy dynamics into a single dimensional score. Multi-feature acoustic stress models show stronger predictive validity than any single feature, with test-retest reliability of r=0.65-0.78 in controlled settings. The Kintsugi DAM platform validation included stress detection as a primary output across their clinical trial datasets.
stress_score.evidence_citation: Menne et al., arXiv 2025; Kintsugi Health, Annals of Family Medicine 2025

mood_score.description: A 0-100 score reflecting overall mood quality inferred from your voice — higher is better.
mood_score.science_summary: Mood valence is derived from a combination of pitch expressiveness, energy, speech rate, and spectral brightness features that collectively differentiate positive from negative emotional states. Acoustic mood estimates correlate moderately with self-reported mood (r=0.35-0.55) in daily diary studies. Longitudinal tracking shows sensitivity to mood episode transitions even before subjective awareness.
mood_score.evidence_citation: Gideon et al., Interspeech 2019; Schuller et al., AVEC 2016

energy_score.description: A 0-100 score reflecting physical and mental energy as expressed through your voice.
energy_score.science_summary: Vocal energy combines RMS amplitude, speech rate, and spectral brightness into an activation-valence energy estimate. Low vocal energy is characteristic of fatigue, depression, and burnout, while high energy corresponds to alertness and engagement. The signal shows meaningful day-level variability in longitudinal monitoring studies.
energy_score.evidence_citation: Schuller et al., AVEC 2013; Alghifari et al., Sensors 2024

calm_score.description: A 0-100 score capturing how calm and regulated your voice sounds — higher is calmer.
calm_score.science_summary: The calm score is derived inversely from stress indicators: high-frequency spectral energy, jitter, voice breaks, and alpha ratio deviations. Calm vocal states correspond to parasympathetic nervous system dominance and are associated with slower speech, more stable pitch, and a favorable HNR. Day-level calm scores correlate with self-reported relaxation and recovery quality.
calm_score.evidence_citation: Menne et al., arXiv 2025; Kim et al., IEEE TAFFC 2018

depression_raw.description: The raw output of the Kintsugi DAM model's depression detection network, before scale mapping.
depression_raw.science_summary: The Kintsugi Digital Affect Measurement (DAM) model was validated in a prospective study of 14,898 participants published in the Annals of Family Medicine (2025). The model achieved AUROC of 0.80 for depression detection compared to PHQ-9 ground truth. The raw score captures the model's continuous confidence output before normalization to the PHQ-9 scale.
depression_raw.evidence_citation: Kintsugi Health, Annals of Family Medicine 2025

anxiety_raw.description: The raw output of the Kintsugi DAM model's anxiety detection network, before scale mapping.
anxiety_raw.science_summary: The DAM anxiety model operates in parallel to the depression model, using overlapping but distinct acoustic feature patterns. Anxiety-linked features include elevated F0, reduced alpha ratio, faster speech rate, and elevated spectral flux. The raw output is a continuous detection confidence score validated against GAD-7 ground truth across the 14,898-participant study.
anxiety_raw.evidence_citation: Kintsugi Health, Annals of Family Medicine 2025

depression_mapped.description: Depression signal mapped to the PHQ-9 clinical scale (0-27), where higher scores indicate greater symptom burden.
depression_mapped.science_summary: The raw DAM output is calibrated to match PHQ-9 score distributions from the validation cohort, enabling direct comparison to clinical thresholds (minimal: 0-4, mild: 5-9, moderate: 10-14, moderately severe: 15-19, severe: 20-27). This mapping does not constitute a clinical diagnosis but provides a clinically interpretable reference frame for tracking within-person changes over time.
depression_mapped.evidence_citation: Kintsugi Health, Annals of Family Medicine 2025; Kroenke et al., J. Gen. Int. Med. 2001

anxiety_mapped.description: Anxiety signal mapped to the GAD-7 clinical scale (0-21), where higher scores indicate greater anxiety burden.
anxiety_mapped.science_summary: Analogous to depression mapping, the anxiety raw score is calibrated against GAD-7 distributions from the validation cohort. GAD-7 thresholds provide clinical context (minimal: 0-4, mild: 5-9, moderate: 10-14, severe: 15-21). The mapped score enables meaningful week-over-week comparisons and pattern tracking for the individual user.
anxiety_mapped.evidence_citation: Kintsugi Health, Annals of Family Medicine 2025; Spitzer et al., Arch. Int. Med. 2006

depression_quantized.description: Depression severity as a categorical level: 0=minimal, 1=mild, 2=moderate, 3=moderately severe, 4=severe.
depression_quantized.science_summary: Derived from PHQ-9 mapped score using standard clinical cut-points. Categorical classification allows for threshold-based notifications and simple trend tracking without requiring the user to interpret continuous scales. Transitions between categories, especially from minimal to mild, are clinically meaningful events that warrant attention.
depression_quantized.evidence_citation: Kroenke et al., J. Gen. Int. Med. 2001; Kintsugi Health, Annals of Family Medicine 2025

anxiety_quantized.description: Anxiety severity as a categorical level: 0=minimal, 1=mild, 2=moderate, 3=severe.
anxiety_quantized.science_summary: Derived from GAD-7 mapped score using standard clinical cut-points. Categorical severity provides a stable, interpretable signal for tracking anxiety burden over time. Week-over-week changes in severity category are more meaningful than single-session readings, given day-to-day variability in vocal expression.
anxiety_quantized.evidence_citation: Spitzer et al., Arch. Int. Med. 2006; Kintsugi Health, Annals of Family Medicine 2025

filler_rate.description: How often you use filler words (um, uh, like) per 100 words — reflects cognitive load and planning difficulty.
filler_rate.science_summary: Filler rate increases reliably under cognitive load and when speech planning is disrupted. Elevated filler usage is observed in anxiety, ADHD, and early cognitive decline. It is one of the most robust linguistic markers of processing difficulty, with consistent findings across languages and populations.
filler_rate.evidence_citation: Clark & Fox Tree, Cognition 2002; Corley & Stewart, J. Mem. Lang. 2008

hedging_score.description: Frequency of hedging phrases (maybe, sort of, I think) — reflects uncertainty and tentativeness in speech.
hedging_score.science_summary: Hedging language increases in states of low confidence, social anxiety, and depressive rumination. Persistent high hedging correlates with trait anxiety and lower self-efficacy. In clinical interviews, hedging patterns distinguish anxious from non-anxious speakers with moderate accuracy.
hedging_score.evidence_citation: Hyland, Hedging in Scientific Research Articles 1998; Pennebaker et al., LIWC Studies 2015

negative_sentiment.description: The proportion of words conveying negative emotions (sad, angry, worried) relative to total words.
negative_sentiment.science_summary: Elevated negative emotion word usage is one of the most replicated linguistic markers of depression and anxiety. LIWC-based analyses across multiple large cohorts show that depressed individuals use 50-80% more negative emotion words than controls. The signal is robust across written and spoken language.
negative_sentiment.evidence_citation: Rude et al., Cognition & Emotion 2004; Tausczik & Pennebaker, Psychol. Methods 2010

disfluency_rate.description: Total disfluencies (false starts, repetitions, corrections) per 100 words.
disfluency_rate.science_summary: Disfluency rate captures speech planning breakdowns beyond fillers, including false starts, word repetitions, and mid-sentence corrections. Elevated disfluency is associated with cognitive overload, anxiety-driven monitoring, and early neurodegenerative changes. It is a complementary marker to filler rate for assessing speech fluency.
disfluency_rate.evidence_citation: Shriberg, Interspeech 2001; Bortfeld et al., Memory & Cognition 2001

lexical_diversity.description: The ratio of unique words to total words — reflects vocabulary breadth and cognitive flexibility.
lexical_diversity.science_summary: Reduced lexical diversity is observed in depression, cognitive decline, and psychomotor retardation. Depressed speakers tend to use a more restricted vocabulary, repeating the same words and phrases. Type-token ratio and its variants (MATTR, MTLD) show moderate correlations with depression severity in clinical samples.
lexical_diversity.evidence_citation: Covington et al., Schizophrenia Research 2005; Bedi et al., npj Schizophrenia 2015

pronoun_i_ratio.description: The proportion of first-person singular pronouns (I, me, my) in your speech — reflects self-focused attention.
pronoun_i_ratio.science_summary: Elevated first-person singular pronoun use is one of the most robust linguistic markers of depression, replicated across 25+ studies and meta-analyses. The effect (d=0.13-0.20) is small but consistent, reflecting the self-focused rumination characteristic of depressive thinking. Rude et al. (2004) and subsequent meta-analyses confirm this as a stable trait-level marker.
pronoun_i_ratio.evidence_citation: Rude et al., Cognition & Emotion 2004; Edwards & Holtzman, Clinical Psych. Science 2017 (meta-analysis)

absolutist_ratio.description: Frequency of absolutist words (always, never, completely, nothing) — reflects cognitive rigidity.
absolutist_ratio.science_summary: Al-Mosaiwi & Johnstone (2018) demonstrated that absolutist thinking words are elevated in depression, anxiety, and suicidal ideation forums compared to controls. The effect was strongest for suicidal ideation (50% more absolutist words). This marker captures the cognitive distortion of black-and-white thinking central to CBT models of depression.

sentiment_valence.description: Overall emotional valence of your speech content — positive values indicate positive tone, negative values indicate negative tone.
sentiment_valence.science_summary: Sentiment valence derived from NLP models captures the overall emotional coloring of speech content. Persistently negative valence correlates with depressive episodes and low mood states. Combined with acoustic mood scoring, linguistic sentiment provides a dual-channel assessment of emotional state that is more robust than either alone.
sentiment_valence.evidence_citation: Eichstaedt et al., PNAS 2018; De Choudhury et al., ICWSM 2013

sentiment_arousal.description: The emotional intensity or activation level expressed through word choice — higher means more emotionally charged language.
sentiment_arousal.science_summary: Arousal captures the intensity dimension of the affect circumplex, independent of valence. High arousal language (excited, terrified, furious) indicates emotional activation, while low arousal (calm, bored, tired) indicates emotional dampening. Depression typically shows reduced arousal, while anxiety shows elevated arousal, making this a useful differentiating marker.
sentiment_arousal.evidence_citation: Russell, J. Pers. Soc. Psych. 1980; Warriner et al., Behavior Research Methods 2013

semantic_coherence.description: How logically connected your sentences are — measures the flow and organization of your thoughts during speech.
semantic_coherence.science_summary: Semantic coherence, measured via sentence embedding similarity, captures disorganized thinking patterns. Reduced coherence is a marker of thought disorder in psychotic conditions and is also observed in severe depression and high cognitive load states. Bedi et al. (2015) showed semantic coherence predicted psychosis onset with 100% accuracy in a small cohort, though larger replications show more moderate effects.
semantic_coherence.evidence_citation: Bedi et al., npj Schizophrenia 2015; Elvevag et al., PLoS ONE 2007
